cataloging_subject.label: Middle English translations and continuations of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon (Rolls Series edited selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The intellectual and content unit selected in the U-M/CME encoding AHB1341.0001.001: John Trevisa's seven-book Middle English translation with its short continuation, an anonymous second seven-book Middle English translation, and William Caxton's adaptation/continuation complex. The alternative Book VI passage from Harley MS 1900 is a zero-count version child of the Trevisa family. The subject is not Higden's complete Latin Polychronicon, the complete nine-volume Rolls Series edition, any manuscript as a physical object, or either digital carrier.

catalog_summary.title: Middle English translations and continuations of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon (Rolls Series edited selection)
catalog_summary.author: Ranulf Higden (Latin source compiler); John Trevisa, an anonymous second translator, and William Caxton (English versions and continuations)
catalog_summary.editor: Churchill Babington and Joseph Rawson Lumby
catalog_summary.date: Trevisa translation completed 1387; anonymous translation probably c. 1432-1460; Caxton adaptation and continuation printed 1482; source edition published 1865-1886

titles.value: Middle English translations and continuations of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon (Rolls Series edited selection)

assertion.notes: A project-supplied title that names the cataloging boundary and avoids treating the three English intellectual families as one medieval work.

titles.value: Polychronicon Ranulphi Higden maonachi Cestrensis; together with the English translations of John Trevisa and of an unknown writer of the fifteenth century.
notes: The spelling 'maonachi' is preserved exactly from the U-M bibliographic title; the printed edition reads 'monachi'.

titles.value: Polychronicon Ranulphi Higden monachi Cestrensis; together with the English translations of John Trevisa and of an unknown writer of the fifteenth century

scope.description: Title of the broader nine-volume source edition from which this edited selection is encoded
Underlying Latin universal chronicle translated or adapted by the three English intellectual families

notes: This does not make Higden the author of the English translations or Caxton's continuation.

assertion.notes: Caxton modernized and adapted Trevisa and supplied the continuation through 1460 represented by the selected late-Book-VII and Book-VIII streams.

scope.description: OCR-derived U-M digital encoding represented by the pinned repository XML

assertion.notes: The intellectual version's inferred translation range is distinct from the sole witness's catalog date of 1450-1474.

form_statements.description: The cataloging subject combines prose chronicle with substantive verse in two of the three count-once families. Trevisa Book I contains a 470-line verse group within 624 Trevisa-family verse lines; the anonymous family contains 211 verse lines, including sustained verse in Book VII. Caxton's 17 verse lines are brief quotations and do not cross the substantive-mixed threshold, so that family remains prose-only.

summaries.value: An edited selection of the English textual tradition of Ranulf Higden's universal chronicle: John Trevisa's complete seven-book translation and short continuation, an independent anonymous seven-book translation, and William Caxton's modernized adaptation and continuation through 1460.

The target has 17 encoded DIV1 streams and 613 DIV2 chapter divisions: seven Trevisa books, seven anonymous-translation books, one Harley MS 1900 Book VI alternative passage, Caxton's selected late Book VII passage, and Caxton's Book VIII continuation. The manifest summarizes those axes in 20 content records: three count-bearing intellectual-family parents plus 17 zero-count structural/version children.

Count once at the intellectual-family level: Trevisa translation plus short continuation; anonymous second translation; Caxton adaptation/continuation complex. Seven-book divisions, Caxton's Book VII and Book VIII streams, 613 chapters, and the Harley MS 1900 alternative are structural or version records and do not increase the intellectual item count.

extent.description: Three count-once intellectual-family items are represented by 17 target DIV1 streams and 613 target DIV2 chapters in the pinned XML. The manifest contains 20 content records: three count-bearing parents and 17 zero-count children. Here `sections` means the 17 target DIV1 stream axis, not the 20 manifest records. All 73 LG elements and 852 L elements are main-stream text rather than NOTE1 apparatus. Trevisa (624 verse lines, including one 470-line group) and the anonymous translation (211 verse lines, including sustained Book VII verse) are mixed; Caxton remains prose-only because its 17 verse lines are brief quotations.

content_parts.label: Trevisa translation and short continuation
content_parts.summary: John Trevisa's seven-book Middle English translation, completed in 1387 for Thomas, Lord Berkeley, including the short continuation printed with it.
notes: Embedded verse is measured on the seven zero-count book children, so the family parent carries the count-once mixed-item metric without duplicating child verse totals.

related_works.title: Polychronicon
notes: The cataloging subject contains English versions and continuations of Higden's Latin chronicle; their distinct relations are modeled in the lineage graph.

evidence.citation: Kyvero Press CME source XML for AHB1341.0001.001 at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c
evidence.locator: HEADER; target DIV1 nodes :3-:19 excluding non-target DIV1 nodes
evidence.summary: Pins the historical U-M-derived encoding and supplies the bibliographic header, production statement, page-image references, edition page milestones, and complete encoded target text.

evidence.citation: XPath count and boundary inspection of pinned AHB1341 XML
evidence.locator: DIV1 nodes AHB1341.0001.001:3 through :19; descendant DIV2, LG, and L counts
evidence.summary: Verifies 17 target DIV1 streams, 613 target DIV2 chapters, 73 verse groups, and 852 verse lines. The 17 streams comprise seven Trevisa books, seven anonymous books, one Harley alternative, and two Caxton streams. The 20-record manifest model adds three count-bearing parents without treating them as extra encoded streams.

evidence.citation: Verse-form audit against the pinned XML and Rolls Series volume I and VIII scans
evidence.locator: vol. I pp. 395-397 and following (Trevisa's 470-line Book I verse); vol. VIII pp. 518-521 (anonymous 15 seven-line royal stanzas); vol. VIII p. 557 (Caxton's brief 17-line Latin quotation)
evidence.summary: Confirms that the encoded LG/L structures are main-stream verse rather than NOTE1 apparatus. Trevisa's family is mixed because Book I contains a sustained 470-line verse section; the anonymous family is mixed because Book VII includes 15 consecutive seven-line royal stanzas in addition to other verse; Caxton remains prose-only because its 17 verse lines are a brief quotation embedded in prose.
notes: The anonymous and Caxton pages were checked in source-cache/ahb1341/rolls-series-41-vol8-1882-ia.pdf; the XML count and inline NOTE1 placement were checked in source-cache/ahb1341/cme-pinned-ahb1341-e1d77c88.xml.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan CME item AHB1341.0001.001
evidence.summary: Supports the current item identity, title, collection context, public-domain statement, and public web-text access.

evidence.citation: Babington and Lumby, eds., Polychronicon Ranulphi Higden monachi Cestrensis, Rolls Series 41, nine volumes, 1865-1886
evidence.summary: Supports edition identity, editors, volume dates, selected printed-page portions, editorial witnesses, translation headings, and continuation boundaries.

evidence.citation: Aino Liira, Paratextuality in Manuscript and Print: Verbal and Visual Presentation of the Middle English Polychronicon (University of Turku, 2020)
evidence.summary: Supports the distinction among Trevisa, anonymous, and Caxton versions and the descriptions of the principal manuscript witnesses.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalogue, Harley MS 2261
evidence.summary: Supports the identity, date, extent, and folio range of the sole manuscript witness to the anonymous second translation.

evidence.citation: CERL ISTC ih00267000, Higden, Ranulf: Polycronicon, translated by John Trevisa, with continuation by William Caxton
evidence.summary: Supports Caxton's 1482 imprint, continuation attribution and date range, identifiers, and holdings context.

open_questions.question: What exact folio in Harley MS 1900 ends the Book VI chapters 15-26 alternative printed on Rolls Series volume VII pages [501]-538?
next_steps: Inspect the manuscript or obtain an authoritative folio-level description; no enquiry has been sent.

open_questions.question: Which physical copies supplied U-M's 5,176 page images, and what current direct image-delivery route, if any, is publicly usable?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections staff to identify scan-copy provenance and current image delivery; no enquiry has been sent.

open_questions.question: Which exact Caxton 1482 copy or copies did the Rolls Series editors use for the selected readings and continuation?
next_steps: Review all Rolls Series editorial introductions and collation sigla against surviving ISTC holdings; no holding institution has been contacted.

open_questions.question: How should the collection-level manual-keying/proofreading account be reconciled with this item's header statement that it was OCR-created without corrections or content editing?
next_steps: Seek item-level production documentation from U-M; no enquiry has been sent.

notes: The 17 target DIV1 streams, 613 DIV2 chapters, three count-once intellectual items, and 20 manifest content records are distinct axes and must not be conflated.

All 20 content parts carry explicit item_count and all five metrics. Count-bearing parents carry one prose-only and two mixed-item classifications; zero-count structural children carry the 73 verse-group and 852 verse-line measurements without double counting.

The lineage schema has no abstract-work or abstract-version entity types. The Latin work and English version nodes are therefore represented explicitly as catalog_description entities, with notes identifying that schema compromise.

The repository source XML, current U-M encoding, current Medictionary reformat, printed volume expressions, digital facsimile carriers, carrier metadata, and physical witnesses are distinct layers. Latin and Modern English in headings, citations, parallel columns, or editorial apparatus are representation details rather than work-discovery language facets.
